**Johannes "Johan/Jan" Boskamp** (born 21 October 1948) is a Dutch former [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") player and manager.

He played the majority of his career for hometown club [Feyenoord](/wiki/Feyenoord "Feyenoord") and Belgian side [RWDM](/wiki/R.W.D._Molenbeek_(1909) "R.W.D. Molenbeek (1909)"), and managed primarily in the Belgian leagues. Currently, he is a regular [sports commentator](/wiki/Sports_commentator "Sports commentator") on two Dutch and Belgian football television programs. He is commonly addressed as "Jan" in the Netherlands and "Johan" in Belgium.

## Playing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johan_Boskamp&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Playing career")]

### Club

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johan_Boskamp&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Club")]

His former clubs as a player include RVV HOV, [Feyenoord](/wiki/Feyenoord_Rotterdam "Feyenoord Rotterdam"), [Holland Sport](/wiki/ADO_Den_Haag "ADO Den Haag"), [RWD Molenbeek](/wiki/R.W.D._Molenbeek_(1909) "R.W.D. Molenbeek (1909)") (with whom he won the Belgian First Division in 1975) and [Lierse](/wiki/Lierse_S.K._(1906) "Lierse S.K. (1906)"). He also won the [1970 Intercontinental Cup](/wiki/1970_Intercontinental_Cup "1970 Intercontinental Cup") with Feyenoord.

Boskamp was voted [Belgian Golden Shoe](/wiki/Belgian_Golden_Shoe "Belgian Golden Shoe") winner in 1975.

### International

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johan_Boskamp&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: International")]

He was part of the [Dutch team](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team") for the [1978 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1978_FIFA_World_Cup "1978 FIFA World Cup"), making one substitute appearance against [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland_national_football_team "Scotland national football team").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Coaching career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Johan_Boskamp&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Coaching career")]

Later, Boskamp became a manager, and coached [Belgian](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") clubs [Lierse](/wiki/Lierse_S.K._(1906) "Lierse S.K. (1906)"), [Dender](/wiki/F._C._Verbroedering_Dender_E.H. "F. C. Verbroedering Dender E.H."), [Beveren](/wiki/K.S.K._Beveren "K.S.K. Beveren"), [Kortrijk](/wiki/K.V._Kortrijk "K.V. Kortrijk"), [Anderlecht](/wiki/R.S.C._Anderlecht "R.S.C. Anderlecht") and [Gent](/wiki/K.A.A._Gent "K.A.A. Gent"). He then moved to [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)") in 1999 to manage [Dinamo Tbilisi](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Tbilisi "FC Dinamo Tbilisi"), as well as the [Georgia national team](/wiki/Georgia_national_football_team "Georgia national football team"). After a return to Belgium with [Genk](/wiki/K.R.C._Genk "K.R.C. Genk"), he moved to the Middle East and managed the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") side [Al Wasl](/wiki/Al_Wasl_FC "Al Wasl FC"), and the [Kuwaiti](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait") club [Kazma](/wiki/Kazma_Sporting_Club "Kazma Sporting Club").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

He became manager of English side [Stoke City](/wiki/Stoke_City_F.C. "Stoke City F.C.") for the [2005–06](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Stoke_City_F.C._season "2005–06 Stoke City F.C. season") season.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-4) Stoke's Icelandic board wanted the club to start mounting a serious attempt at gaining promotion to the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League"), and so decided a change in style was required, with Boskamp replacing [Tony Pulis](/wiki/Tony_Pulis "Tony Pulis"). He brought in a number of foreign players, which included [Carl Hoefkens](/wiki/Carl_Hoefkens "Carl Hoefkens"), [Hannes Sigurðsson](/wiki/Hannes_Sigur%C3%B0sson "Hannes Sigurðsson"), [Junior N'Galula](/wiki/Gabriel_N%27Galula "Gabriel N'Galula") and [Martin Kolář](/wiki/Martin_Kol%C3%A1%C5%99 "Martin Kolář"), as well as domestically-based players such as [Marlon Broomes](/wiki/Marlon_Broomes "Marlon Broomes"), [Paul Gallagher](/wiki/Paul_Gallagher_(footballer) "Paul Gallagher (footballer)"), [Mamady Sidibe](/wiki/Mamady_Sidibe "Mamady Sidibe"), [Peter Sweeney](/wiki/Peter_Henry_Sweeney "Peter Henry Sweeney") and [Luke Chadwick](/wiki/Luke_Chadwick "Luke Chadwick"). He also broke the club record transfer fee with a £950,000 signing of [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège") striker [Sambégou Bangoura](/wiki/Samb%C3%A9gou_Bangoura "Sambégou Bangoura"). However, results were often poor, and after a number of heavy home defeats to [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C."), [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") and [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City_F.C. "Cardiff City F.C."), supporters began asking questions. Bangoura then went on a good run of form scoring seven goals in six matches, as Stoke won six matches in November and December to give them a platform to build on going into the new year.[[4]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-4) But, in one of those wins away at [Coventry City](/wiki/Coventry_City_F.C. "Coventry City F.C."), Boskamp and his assistant [Jan de Koning](/wiki/Jan_de_Koning_(footballer) "Jan de Koning (footballer)") and director of football [John Rudge](/wiki/John_Rudge "John Rudge") were involved in an argument, which led to Boskamp almost resigning.[[4]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Stoke began 2006 in terrible form, winning just one match in ten, and scoring a mere six goals in that time.[[4]](#cite_note-The_Sentinel_review-4) Bangoura had been away on international duty with Guinea, and failed to return to the club at the agreed date, which caused the shortage of goals; with Stoke's season fizzling out, with no chance of promotion, Boskamp was not offered a new contract by [Gunnar Gíslason](/wiki/Gunnar_G%C3%ADslason_(businessman) "Gunnar Gíslason (businessman)").[[6]](#cite_note-6) With the Icelandic board failing to gain promotion to the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League"), and with debts now at around £5 million, chairman [Gunnar Gíslason](/wiki/Gunnar_G%C3%ADslason_(businessman) "Gunnar Gíslason (businessman)") put the club up for sale, and he sold the club back to former chairman [Peter Coates](/wiki/Peter_Coates "Peter Coates").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Coates then re-appointed [Tony Pulis](/wiki/Tony_Pulis "Tony Pulis") as manager, who had spent the season with [Plymouth Argyle](/wiki/Plymouth_Argyle_F.C. "Plymouth Argyle F.C.").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Boskamp was then briefly manager at [Standard Liège](/wiki/Standard_Li%C3%A8ge "Standard Liège") in 2006.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In November 2007, he became coach of another [Belgian](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") club: [Dender](/wiki/FCV_Dender_EH "FCV Dender EH"). On 19 May 2009, he quit [Dender](/wiki/FCV_Dender_EH "FCV Dender EH") after an argument with his coaching assistant Patrick Asselman, who was subsequently named as his replacement.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In June 2009, Boskamp signed with [Beveren](/wiki/SK_Beveren "SK Beveren"),[[12]](#cite_note-12) but was sacked in December 2009 after poor results.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In June 2024, Boskamp put an end to his career as an analyst and Standaard Uitgeverij published in collaboration with Boskamp's partner the autobiography *Boskamp - Mijn leven* (*Boskamp - My Life*).
